activities.title: Picture Questions
activities.summary: Turn an image or object into clear questions before asking AI.
activities.goal: Practice asking one simple question at a time and checking whether the answer matches what you can see.
steps: Pick one object, drawing, or photo with an adult nearby.
Say what you notice before asking AI anything.
Ask one question and compare the answer with the object.
Mark anything that sounds confusing or too certain.
prompts: I see ____. What are three safe questions I can ask about it?
Explain this like I am seven, and tell me one thing I should check.
Give me a short answer, then ask me one follow-up question.

activities.title: Story Studio
activities.summary: Build a short story while keeping names, places, and feelings clear.
activities.goal: Use AI as a helper for imagination without letting it take over the child's ideas.
steps: Choose a main character and a simple problem.
Ask AI for three story paths, then choose one.
Write the ending yourself before asking for feedback.
Check that the story stays kind and age-appropriate.
prompts: Help me make three story ideas about a character who learns ____.
Ask me questions before you write the next part.
Make my sentence clearer, but keep my idea.

activities.title: Question Lab
activities.summary: Rewrite vague questions into focused prompts with success criteria.
activities.goal: Learn that better inputs produce more useful AI outputs.
steps: Write the first question exactly as it comes to mind.
Add the audience, format, and what a good answer should include.
Ask AI for an answer and a checklist of assumptions.
Revise the prompt once and compare the two answers.
prompts: Turn this question into a better prompt: ____.
Answer in five bullets for a student in Year 5, and list two assumptions.
What information is missing from my question?

activities.title: Fact Check Sprint
activities.summary: Separate facts, guesses, and opinions in an AI answer.
activities.goal: Build the habit of checking claims instead of accepting fluent text.
steps: Ask AI for a short explanation about a school topic.
Highlight claims that sound factual.
Choose two claims to verify with a trusted source.
Rewrite the answer with labels for facts, guesses, and opinions.
prompts: Explain ____ in 120 words and label any uncertain parts.
List which parts of this answer need checking: ____.
Give me search terms I can use to verify this claim.

activities.title: Design Critic
activities.summary: Use AI to improve posters, slides, and project plans.
activities.goal: Practice asking for specific feedback and choosing which advice to ignore.
steps: Describe the project and its audience.
Ask AI for feedback on clarity, layout, and missing details.
Pick only two changes to make.
Explain why you accepted or rejected each suggestion.
prompts: Review this project idea for clarity and tell me the top two improvements.
Give feedback as a kind teacher, not as the person doing the work.
What should I keep unchanged?

activities.title: Source Map
activities.summary: Plan what evidence a serious AI-assisted answer needs.
activities.goal: Connect AI output to verifiable sources and clear uncertainty labels.
steps: Define the claim, audience, and decision the answer supports.
Ask AI for a source plan before asking for a final answer.
Check whether each source type is primary, secondary, or opinion.
Create a final answer that names what is confirmed and what is uncertain.
prompts: Before answering, make a source plan for this question: ____.
Separate confirmed facts, interpretations, and open questions.
What would make this answer unreliable?

activities.title: Prompt Debugger
activities.summary: Diagnose why an AI answer is shallow, wrong, or off-task.
activities.goal: Treat prompting as an iterative debugging process.
steps: Save the original prompt and answer.
Identify whether the failure is scope, context, format, or evidence.
Rewrite only the weakest part of the prompt.
Compare both outputs against the original goal.
prompts: Diagnose why this answer missed the mark: ____.
Rewrite my prompt without making it longer than 80 words.
Create a rubric for judging the next answer.

guardrails: Use age-appropriate prompts and keep an adult in the loop for younger learners.
Do not enter private names, addresses, school IDs, passwords, or photos without permission.
Ask AI to label uncertainty, then verify important claims with trusted sources.
Treat AI as a coach, not the owner of the student's work.
Stop and ask an adult when an answer feels unsafe, personal, or too persuasive.
